participants.reasoning: The extracted participant description matches the official eligibility criteria and total sample size, with no conflicting details about the included neonates.
participants.extracted: Thirty-four neonates admitted to a level II neonatal intensive care unit affiliated with Tehran University of Medical Sciences in Tehran, Iran, were enrolled between January and May 2013. Eligible infants were 29 to 37 weeks' gestational age, weighed at least 1200 g, had an endotracheal tube, and had no congenital anomalies, seizures, chest tubes, or intracranial hemorrhage greater than grade II. Infants were also excluded if they had received opiates or sedatives within 4 hours before the intervention or any painful procedure within 30 minutes before the study. The sample included both male and female infants, mostly 1 to 7 days old, with many born by cesarean section, and the infants served as their own controls in a crossover design.

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official facilitated tucking intervention, suctioning context, and environmental controls, with no substantive discrepancies.
interventions.extracted: Facilitated tucking position was used during endotracheal suctioning as a non-pharmacological pain-relief intervention. In this study, the neonate was placed on his or her side with the back gently flexed, the legs brought up into flexion at an angle greater than 90 degrees, the shoulders constrained up to 90 degrees, and the researcher’s hands placed over the head close to the mouth or on the neonate’s face. The intervention was applied during suctioning, while the comparison condition was suctioning without this support. Environmental stimuli such as light and sound were minimized and kept similar across conditions, and the two suctioning episodes were separated by at least two hours.

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted text omits several official details and differs on timing, since the official outcome specifies PIPP recorded immediately after suction with heart rate and SaO2 measured 30 seconds before and after suctioning, whereas the extraction says baseline values were recorded 15 seconds before suctioning.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure behavioral pain during endotracheal suctioning in premature neonates using the Premature Infant Pain Profile (PIPP). The PIPP is a composite procedural pain scale incorporating heart rate, oxygen saturation, facial actions (including closing the eyes, nasolabial furrow/nose-lip-chin, and brow bulge), gestational age, and behavioral state; total scores range from 0 to 21, with higher scores indicating greater pain. Pain was assessed at the time of suctioning, using baseline heart rate and oxygen saturation recorded 15 seconds before suctioning and facial/behavioral observations immediately after suctioning.
